Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers know design patterns as vocabulary for communication, not as mandatory architecture.

Key distinctions:
• The value of knowing patterns is recognizing WHEN a problem calls for one — not applying them preemptively
• Every pattern has a cost (indirection, complexity) — justify it with a real extensibility need
• In interview scenarios, explain WHY you chose the pattern and what alternative you rejected
• The most commonly asked patterns in Senior interviews: Strategy, Observer, Template Method, Builder, Factory
• Framework patterns (Dependency Injection, Proxy, Template Method) are more relevant than GoF trivia

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
Patterns in TRAB logistics:

• Strategy: different routing algorithms (shortest, cheapest, greenest) selected per delivery type
• Observer: delivery status changes notify warehouse, driver app, customer portal, and analytics
• Template Method: document generation (delivery note, invoice, return label) — same workflow, different content
• Builder: complex Route objects with many optional parameters (time windows, vehicle constraints, priority)
• Factory: creating appropriate Carrier implementation based on delivery destination and service level

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to use patterns:

• When the simple solution (if/else, direct method call) is clear and unlikely to change
• When introducing a pattern for a single implementation (no variation = no pattern needed)
• When the team doesn't recognize the pattern — it becomes obfuscation, not communication
• When you're adding patterns to impress rather than to solve a real extensibility problem
